topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun beat down, making the air shimmer above Mrs. Henderson’s overgrown patch of lawn. I stood on the edge of my own property, clutching the worn fabric of my cardigan. When Mr. Peterson shuffled over, his smile seemed too wide, his eyes darting everywhere but directly at me. "Excuse me," he began, his voice pitched a little too high, "I was wondering if I might possibly borrow a little bit of your space? Just a corner, for a small herb garden." My throat tightened. I looked down at my shoes, the pavement suddenly feeling too hot, too exposed. The thought of letting anyone into my carefully ordered sanctuary felt like handing over a key to a locked room. I managed a small, jerky nod, the sound barely escaping my lips.

[story 2]

I was watering my prize-winning roses when the sound of his footsteps approached. He stopped a respectful distance away, his hands clasped tightly in front of him. "Hello," he said, his tone unusually soft, almost hesitant. "I see you have a lovely yard. Would it be at all possible for me to use a section? Just a small area for some vegetables." My breath hitched. My hands, which had been steady holding the hose, started to tremble slightly. I felt a sudden, intense urge to retreat, to become invisible behind the dense foliage of my hedges. I swallowed hard, trying to find a steady rhythm for my voice. "Well," I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy, "I suppose I could look at it."

[story 3]

The request hung in the air, heavy and awkward. When the neighbor finally spoke, his gaze kept flickering toward the fence separating our properties, never quite settling on my face. "I was hoping," he continued, shifting his weight from one foot to the other, "to utilize a small patch of your yard for gardening purposes." A cold knot formed in my stomach, tightening with every word he spoke. I fiddled with a loose thread on my sleeve, my gaze fixed intently on a distant bird. The silence stretched, filled only by the buzzing of insects, which suddenly sounded deafening. I felt a distinct pressure behind my eyes, a need to simply dissolve into the background noise. "Let me check my schedule," I murmured, the words sounding far more strained than intended.
